items.title: SpeechRecognitionManager: no deinit cleanup — audio engine leaks if deallocated while recording
items.intent: Audio resources must be released on dealloc — a live mic after the editor closes is a privacy violation and a resource bug
items.description: SpeechRecognitionManager has no deinit. If the instance is deallocated while state is .recording or .starting, AVAudioEngine and SFSpeechRecognitionTask are never stopped or cancelled. The microphone tap remains active and the OS audio session is not released. stopRecording() exists but is not called on dealloc.
steps: In Sources/EMEditor/SpeechRecognitionManager.swift, add deinit: if audioEngine \!= nil { audioEngine?.stop(); audioEngine?.inputNode.removeTap(onBus: 0); audioEngine = nil; recognitionTask?.cancel(); recognitionTask = nil; state = .idle }
Verify the deinit executes on the main actor (SpeechRecognitionManager is @MainActor — Swift guarantees deinit runs on the actor's executor for actor-isolated types)

Test: in EMAITests or EMEditorTests, create SpeechRecognitionManager, set audioEngine to a non-nil AVAudioEngine stub, then nil the manager reference and verify via weak var that the instance deallocates and audioEngine cleanup path was hit (use a spy/subclass or test hook)
acceptanceCriteria: Deallocating SpeechRecognitionManager while audioEngine is non-nil stops the engine and removes the tap
Deallocating SpeechRecognitionManager while recognitionTask is non-nil cancels the task
Unit test passes verifying dealloc path cleans up audio resources
items.groomingNotes: File: Sources/EMEditor/SpeechRecognitionManager.swift. STEPS: (1) Add deinit to SpeechRecognitionManager: check if audioEngine != nil — if so, call audioEngine?.stop(), audioEngine?.inputNode.removeTap(onBus: 0), audioEngine = nil, recognitionTask?.cancel(), recognitionTask = nil. Set state = .idle to prevent stale observers firing. (2) Because SpeechRecognitionManager is @MainActor, deinit runs on the main actor — no threading concern. (3) Test in EMAITests or EMEditorTests: create a SpeechRecognitionManager, call startRecording() (mock AVAudioEngine or skip to state = .recording manually), then assign manager = nil — verify via weak reference that it is deallocated and audioEngine is nil (add a teardown observable property for testing). (4) swift build must pass.

items.title: ImageLoader: in-flight Task.detached loads not cancelled on dealloc — remote image tasks outlive the loader
items.intent: Network requests must not outlive the component that issued them — dangling URLSession tasks waste bandwidth and complicate memory analysis
items.description: loadImageIfNeeded fires Task.detached without storing the Task handle. When ImageLoader is deallocated (e.g., document closed), any in-flight URLSession downloads for remote images continue running with no way to cancel them. The [weak self] guard prevents crashes, but the URLSession download itself is not cancelled, wasting bandwidth for potentially large remote images.
steps: In Sources/EMEditor/ImageLoader.swift, add private var tasks: [URL: Task<Void, Never>] = [:] to the class
In loadImageIfNeeded, change 'Task.detached { ... }' to 'let task = Task.detached { ... }' and store: tasks[url] = task
Inside the Task body in the MainActor.run block, after setting cache entry, add: self.tasks.removeValue(forKey: url)
Add deinit to ImageLoader: tasks.values.forEach { $0.cancel() }; tasks.removeAll()
acceptanceCriteria: Deallocating ImageLoader cancels all stored in-flight tasks
Task handle is removed from tasks dictionary when load completes (no unbounded growth)
Existing load-deduplication via inFlightURLs is unaffected
items.groomingNotes: User impact: On slow connections, closing a document before images load leaves silent download tasks running in the background — burning mobile data for images the user will never see. This is a silent cost, not a crash, so users cannot report it. Fix is narrow (ImageLoader.swift only). The three AC are already implementation-testable; no user-visible behavior changes. Confirm intent matches product value: 'zero resource waste when document closes'.

items.title: CloudAPIProvider: no retry on transient network failure — users get immediate error on brief connectivity drop
items.intent: A momentary network blip must not surface as an AI failure — one silent retry is the minimum bar for a production cloud service
items.description: CloudAPIProvider maps URLError.timedOut and URLError.networkConnectionLost to EMError.ai(.inferenceTimeout/.cloudUnavailable) with no retry. A brief connectivity drop during generation causes an immediate user-visible failure. A single retry with a 1-2s delay would handle most transient failures transparently.
steps: In Sources/EMAI/CloudAPIProvider.swift, identify the URLSession data task call inside generate(). Wrap it in a retry loop: func withRetry<T>(maxAttempts: Int, delay: Duration, _ body: () async throws -> T) async throws -> T — attempt body(), on URLError.networkConnectionLost or URLError.timedOut wait delay then retry, on final attempt rethrow. Use maxAttempts: 2, delay: .seconds(1.5).
Place the retry helper as a private free function in the same file (not a new file — it is used in one place)
Do NOT retry on: URLError.notConnectedToInternet (device is offline — retry is futile), HTTP 4xx errors (client errors — retry is futile), EMError.ai(.cancelled) (user cancelled — do not retry)

Test in EMCloudTests: mock URLSession to fail once with URLError.networkConnectionLost then succeed — verify generate() returns success, not error. Also verify that URLError.notConnectedToInternet is NOT retried (expect immediate failure).
acceptanceCriteria: Single URLError.networkConnectionLost or timedOut error during generation triggers one silent retry after 1.5s
Second consecutive transient failure surfaces as EMError.ai(.cloudUnavailable) to the caller
URLError.notConnectedToInternet is not retried — immediate failure
Unit tests verify retry path and non-retry path
items.groomingNotes: User impact: iOS users on cellular frequently hit brief connectivity drops (subway, elevator, building handoff). Today, a 1-second blip during an AI rewrite shows the user an error banner and loses their work-in-progress — they must re-invoke the action manually. One silent retry converts this from a frustrating visible failure to a seamless experience. Scope is intentionally narrow: only two transient error codes are retried; offline and server errors fail immediately. No new UI needed. Priority is correct at 'should' — affects all cloud AI users but is not a blocking defect.
